quick_facts.label: What ITI training is
quick_facts.value: Industrial Training Institutes (ITIs) deliver Craftsmen Training Scheme (CTS) vocational courses through government and private institutes; DGT sets national standards and certification arrangements, while States/UTs administer institutes and admissions.

quick_facts.label: Entry requirement
quick_facts.value: Eligibility is trade-specific. Many engineering trades, including Fitter, require Class 10 with Science and Mathematics (or an equivalent/vocational subject in the sector), while some CTS trades accept Class 8 or Class 10 without that subject combination.

quick_facts.label: Course length
quick_facts.value: CTS trade durations currently include 6 months (600 hours), one year (1,200 hours), and two years (2,400 hours); the exact duration depends on the selected trade.

quick_facts.label: Certification
quick_facts.value: After completing a CTS trade and passing the All India Trade Test (AITT), a trainee receives a National Trade Certificate (NTC). A National Apprenticeship Certificate (NAC) is a later, separate outcome after completing trade apprenticeship training and passing the apprenticeship AITT.

quick_facts.label: Trade choice
quick_facts.value: DGT’s CTS catalogue lists 169 NSQF-compliant trades across engineering, non-engineering and Divyang categories. Examples include Electrician, Fitter, Welder and Computer Operator & Programming Assistant (COPA), but availability varies by institute and State/UT.

sections.heading: Who can enter an ITI course?
paragraphs: ITI is not a single course with one national entry rule: CTS eligibility is set trade by trade. For many technical trades, Class 10 with Science and Mathematics is required; for example, DGT lists this requirement for Fitter. Other trades have Class 8 or general Class 10 entry requirements. Applicants should therefore check the exact DGT trade entry requirement and their State/UT admission notice rather than relying only on the broad label “ITI after Class 10.”

Admission is administered by the relevant State Government or Union Territory administration. State/UT notices can additionally specify age, domicile, reservation, document-verification and counselling conditions.
bullets: For a Class 10 student seeking an engineering trade, confirm whether Science and Mathematics are compulsory for that trade.
Choose an NCVT-affiliated CTS unit and verify that the institute is offering the trade in the intended admission session.
Treat trade-specific eligibility and the State/UT prospectus as controlling documents for admission.

sections.heading: How long does ITI take?
paragraphs: The familiar one- or two-year ITI route is the main pathway for many trades, but CTS also includes six-month courses. DGT’s current course catalogue identifies courses as 600-hour, 1,200-hour or 2,400-hour programmes, corresponding to six months, one year and two years respectively.

For restructured one-year CTS courses, DGT specifies 1,200 learning hours plus 150 hours of mandatory on-the-job training or group project work. Two-year courses are listed at 2,400 hours; practical structure can vary by trade and institute.
bullets: One year: 1,200 hours, plus mandatory OJT/group-project provision in the restructured curriculum.

sections.heading: What students learn
paragraphs: CTS training is trade-led and combines hands-on trade practical work with trade theory and employability skills. The detailed learning outcomes, tools, safety content and assessment components are published in DGT’s individual trade curricula; these should be reviewed before selecting a trade because an Electrician, Fitter, Welder and COPA programme lead to materially different work skills.

The CTS framework has been aligned to NSQF levels. DGT also provides for on-the-job training/project work within the restructured curriculum. Some affiliated ITIs may offer the Dual System of Training, in which industry exposure is integrated into the CTS course; this is not automatically available at every ITI or in every trade.
bullets: Trade practical and trade theory are the core of the programme.
Employability skills form a common curriculum component.
Course content, NSQF level, duration and minimum qualification can be checked in DGT’s CTS course catalogue.
Dual System of Training can provide structured industry exposure where the ITI and an industry partner offer it.

sections.heading: How admission works
paragraphs: There is no single all-India ITI admission process for all institutes. DGT states that national policy, training standards, examinations and certification are central responsibilities, while day-to-day ITI administration—including admissions—rests with the respective State/UT. Applicants should use the relevant State/UT vocational-training or skill-development admission portal and the institute’s official notice for the current session.

Before applying, verify the specific institute and unit, the selected trade’s entry qualification and duration, seat availability, whether the unit is affiliated, and the current State/UT counselling or merit procedure. Session dates, application fees, reservation rules and document requirements are variable and should not be assumed from another State.
bullets: Start with the State/UT ITI admission authority, not an unofficial aggregator.
Cross-check the trade in DGT’s CTS catalogue.
Use NCVT MIS/DGT-linked resources to verify the course and institute information where available.

sections.heading: What comes after ITI?
paragraphs: The immediate formal outcome of CTS is the National Trade Certificate, not the NAC: trainees who pass the CTS All India Trade Test receive an NTC. The NTC is recognized for recruitment to relevant posts and services in Central/State government and private establishments, but every employer or recruitment examination sets its own trade, age, physical, category and other eligibility conditions.

A common progression is trade apprenticeship. Under the National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme information published by MSDE, apprenticeship for ITI pass-outs in designated trades ordinarily lasts one year. On completion of apprenticeship training, candidates may take the apprenticeship AITT; passing it leads to the National Apprenticeship Certificate (NAC).

An ITI/NTC can also support progression toward instructor training. DGT’s Craft Instructor Training Scheme (CITS) lists NTC or NAC as eligible qualifications for relevant CITS trades, alongside diploma/degree routes where applicable. CITS is therefore a trade-specific pathway toward becoming a vocational instructor, not an automatic next step for every ITI graduate.

Employment and self-employment are also intended CTS outcomes. For progression to Polytechnic Diploma, Armed Forces recruitment, RRB ALP, DRDO CEPTAM or other technical recruitment, candidates must check the live official notification: an ITI/NTC alone does not establish eligibility across all posts, and accepted trades and requirements vary by recruiting body and year.
bullets: Pass CTS AITT → National Trade Certificate (NTC).
Undertake a registered trade apprenticeship → pass apprenticeship AITT → National Apprenticeship Certificate (NAC).
Apply for trade-relevant technician, production, maintenance, service or self-employment opportunities.
Consider CITS where the candidate holds the required trade-relevant NTC/NAC and meets that course’s conditions.
Check separate, current official notifications for diploma admission and government recruitment pathways.
